# Worktrees, Submodules, and Sparse-Checkout

The three tools in this chapter solve real problems, but they are not
part of most research workflows, and reaching for them when a simpler
option exists is a common way to create work for yourself. This chapter
is deliberately blunt about that. Each section shows what the tool does,
on the running example, and then says plainly when it is worth the extra
complexity and when it is not.

A one-line summary before the detail: worktrees are genuinely useful
and cheap, and worth knowing; submodules are powerful and painful, and
worth avoiding unless you specifically need them; sparse-checkout only
matters for repositories far larger than a typical paper. The decision
table at the end of the chapter makes the same point in one place.

All commands were run against throwaway clones of the running example's
`baseline-4commits` tag. Paths are shown with your home directory
abbreviated to `~`.

## Worktrees: two branches checked out at once {.tier-advanced}

A normal repository has one working directory, so you can only have one
branch checked out at a time. Switching branches means stashing or
committing whatever you are in the middle of. A **worktree** lets one
repository have several working directories, each on its own branch,
all sharing the same history and object store.

The case where this earns its keep in research is concrete. You are
deep in an analysis on `main` when referee comments arrive, and you need
a clean copy on a `revision` branch to start addressing them, without
disturbing the work in progress. Instead of stashing, add a worktree.

```bash
$ git worktree add ../revision -b revision
Preparing worktree (new branch 'revision')
HEAD is now at dcb18de Note headline result in README
```

You now have two folders. `git worktree list` shows both, each pinned
to its branch.

```bash
$ git worktree list
~/research/minwage-did  dcb18de [main]
~/research/revision     dcb18de [revision]
```

The two directories are independent checkouts but one repository: a
commit you make in either appears in the shared history immediately.

```bash
$ git -C ../revision branch --show-current
revision
$ git branch --show-current
main
```

When the revision work is merged, remove the worktree. This deletes the
extra directory but keeps all the commits, which are part of the shared
history.

```bash
$ git worktree remove ../revision
$ git worktree list
~/research/minwage-did  dcb18de [main]
```

::: {.callout-tip .recovery title="RECOVERY"}
A worktree is far safer than the alternatives it replaces. Compared to
stashing (easy to forget, see Chapter 7) or keeping a second full clone
(which drifts out of sync and needs its own pushes and pulls), a
worktree shares one history, so there is nothing to reconcile. The
other common use is running a long job: let a slow estimation run in one
worktree on `main` while you keep editing on a feature branch in
another, with no risk of changing the files underneath the running job.
:::

**When it is worth it.** Often. Worktrees are low-risk and solve a real
daily annoyance. If you find yourself stashing constantly, or keeping a
"clean copy" clone by hand, switch to worktrees.

## Submodules: one repo pinned inside another {.tier-advanced}

A **submodule** embeds one Git repository inside another at a pinned
commit. The outer repository does not store the inner repository's
files; it stores a reference (a "gitlink") to one exact commit of it.
The motivating case is a piece of code or data you maintain separately
and want to reuse across projects: a shared codebook, a cleaning
library, a set of variable definitions.

Here a shared codebook lives in its own repository, and we embed it in
the running example.

```bash
$ git -c protocol.file.allow=always submodule add ../shared-codebook external/codebook
Cloning into '~/research2/minwage-did/external/codebook'...
done.
```

(The `protocol.file.allow` flag is only needed because this demo adds a
submodule from a local path; with a normal GitHub URL you would not use
it.) Git staged two things: a `.gitmodules` file recording where the
submodule comes from, and the pinned pointer itself.

```bash
$ git status --short
A  .gitmodules
A  external/codebook
$ cat .gitmodules
[submodule "external/codebook"]
	path = external/codebook
	url = ../shared-codebook
```

The crucial detail is what the outer repository actually stores. After
committing, the entry for the submodule is not a directory of files but
a single commit reference, mode `160000`.

```bash
$ git submodule status
 43b0a6d53daf74a057481c5f9dbd2b26fa21c4e0 external/codebook (heads/main)
$ git ls-tree HEAD -- external/codebook
160000 commit 43b0a6d53daf74a057481c5f9dbd2b26fa21c4e0	external/codebook
```

That pin is the whole point and the whole problem. The point: your
project records the *exact* version of the shared codebook it was built
against, so the dependency is reproducible. The problem: that pin does
not move on its own, and keeping it correct is manual work that trips up
collaborators.

::: {.callout-warning .pitfall title="PITFALL"}
Submodules have sharp edges that cause real data losses and confusion:

- A fresh `git clone` of the outer repo leaves submodule directories
  **empty**. Collaborators must clone with `--recurse-submodules` (or
  run `git submodule update --init` afterwards), and many will not, then
  report that "the code is missing."
- Inside a submodule you are on a **detached HEAD** at the pinned
  commit. Commits made there without first checking out a branch are
  easy to lose (Chapter 17).
- Updating the pin is two commits in two repositories: push the inner
  repo first, then commit the moved pointer in the outer repo. Push them
  out of order and your collaborators get a pin to a commit they cannot
  fetch.

None of this is a bug; it is the cost of the exact-version guarantee.
:::

**When it is worth it.** Rarely, for most researchers. Use a submodule
only when you genuinely maintain a shared component in its own repo,
need an exact-version pin across several projects, and control both
sides. If you just want to *use* someone's library, install it as a
normal dependency through your environment manager (Chapter 8): that is
what `uv`, `renv`, and `conda` are for, and it is far less fragile. If
you only need a snapshot of some shared files, copying them in (with a
note on their origin and version) is often the honest, low-drama choice.

## Sparse-checkout: only part of a repository on disk {.tier-advanced}

**Sparse-checkout** puts only a chosen subset of a repository's files in
your working directory, while the full history stays in the repository.
It exists for large repositories where checking out everything is slow
or wasteful. The running example is tiny, so this is a demonstration of
mechanics, not a real need.

```bash
$ ls
README.md
output
report.qmd
scripts
```

Turn on cone-mode sparse-checkout (the modern, recommended form) and
restrict the working tree to the `scripts` directory.

```bash
$ git sparse-checkout init --cone
$ git sparse-checkout set scripts
$ ls
README.md
report.qmd
scripts
```

Cone mode keeps the top-level files and the directories you name, and
removes the others (`output/` is gone from disk). Nothing was deleted
from the repository, though: Git still tracks every file in `output/`.

```bash
$ git sparse-checkout list
scripts
$ git ls-files output/
output/tables/did_main.txt
```

Restoring the full working tree is one command.

```bash
$ git sparse-checkout disable
$ ls
README.md
output
report.qmd
scripts
```

::: {.callout-important .reproducibility title="REPRODUCIBILITY"}
Sparse-checkout changes only which files are *on your disk*, never which
files are *in the repository* or its history. A collaborator who clones
normally still gets everything. This makes it safe for reproducibility:
it is a local convenience, not an edit to the project. The related tool
for an oversized *history* (as opposed to an oversized working tree) is
a partial or shallow clone, for example `git clone --filter=blob:none`,
which defers downloading old file contents until needed.
:::

**When it is worth it.** Almost never for a single paper, whose repo is
small by construction (data and outputs are git-ignored, per Chapters 6
and 8). It earns its place in a large shared monorepo, or a repository
that unavoidably contains many big tracked files, where you only work in
one corner. If your research repository is large enough to need
sparse-checkout, that is usually a sign something belongs outside Git
(Chapter 8), not a sign you need this tool.

## Choosing among them

| Tool | Use it when | Prefer instead |
|---|---|---|
| Worktree | You need two branches open at once, or a clean copy without stashing | A second worktree beats a second clone or a deep stash stack |
| Submodule | You maintain a shared component in its own repo and need an exact-version pin across projects | A package via `uv`/`renv`/`conda`, or a copied-in snapshot with a recorded version |
| Sparse-checkout | A repo is too large to check out in full and you work in one part | Keeping the repo small in the first place; a partial clone for huge history |

The pattern across the row is the same: the simpler alternative is
right far more often than the advanced tool, and the advanced tool is
right when its one specific guarantee (a second branch in place, an
exact pin, a partial working tree) is exactly what you need.

## What you can now do

You can open two branches at once with a worktree, embed and pin a
shared repository with a submodule (and you know why you usually should
not), and trim a working tree to a subset with cone-mode
sparse-checkout. More importantly, you can tell when none of them is the
right tool, which for everyday single-paper research is most of the
time.

## Try it yourself {.unnumbered}

Work in a throwaway clone of the running example.

1. Add a worktree on a new `revision` branch with `git worktree add
   ../revision -b revision`. Make a commit in each directory, then run
   `git log` in both and confirm the histories share a base but diverge.
   Remove the worktree and confirm the commits survive.
2. Create a tiny second repository (one CSV, one commit) and add it to
   your clone as a submodule under `external/`. Inspect `.gitmodules`
   and `git submodule status`. Then clone your outer repo *without*
   `--recurse-submodules` and observe the empty submodule directory:
   this is the failure your collaborators will hit.
3. Turn on cone-mode sparse-checkout and reduce the working tree to
   `scripts/`. Confirm with `git ls-files` that `output/` is still
   tracked even though it is no longer on disk. Re-enable the full tree
   with `git sparse-checkout disable`.
4. For each of the three, write one sentence on whether your current
   project would actually benefit from it. For most readers, the honest
   answer for at least two of the three will be "no," and recognizing
   that is the skill this chapter is teaching.
